preFix.title: Assessing and refurbishing second-hand furniture
preFix.subtitle: Inspecting for structural problems, stripping old finish, and re-finishing a solid-wood piece
preFix.excerpt: A solid-wood chair, table, or chest of drawers from a charity shop or auction for 20 to 80 pounds can be refurbished to a better-than-new finish for 20 to 50 pounds in materials. This guide covers the inspection criteria that separate a worthwhile piece from an irreparable one, the stripping sequence for paint or varnish, and the finishing options.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP Valuing Our Clothes and furniture longevity research (2018). Furniture Re-use Network guidance on furniture quality assessment. Historic England guidance on stripping and finishing historic timber (adapted for modern re-use context).

content.text: A solid-wood chair from the 1960s was made from timber that is now 70 to 80 years old. That timber is denser and more stable than the fast-grown wood in equivalent flat-pack furniture today. The

argument for refurbishing it is practical as well as environmental. Older solid wood is structurally superior to modern equivalents at the same price. The refurbishment skills also transfer to other repairs.

Structure: sit on the chair or lean on the table and apply lateral load. Some movement is normal in old glued joints; severe racking indicates multiple failed joints that need re-gluing throughout. A 2 to 4 hour job.

Wood type: tap the piece and listen. Solid wood has a resonant thud; hollow veneered MDF or chipboard sounds flat and hollow. If it sounds hollow on the flat surfaces but solid at the edges. It is veneered board: it can still be finished but cannot be re-jointed or repaired if structural joints fail.

Woodworm: inspect for small round holes and fine dust in the holes. Fresh holes have sharp edges and cream-coloured dust; old holes are grey-edged and dustless. Old holes indicate past infestation that is now inactive. Active holes should be treated with woodworm killer before bringing the piece indoors.

Irreparable faults: warped or split tops on tables that are more than 3 mm out of flat are very difficult to correct without specialist woodworking equipment. Missing legs or structural elements are workable if the hardware shop stocks the right profile. Missing carved or moulded sections require specialist repairs.

Painted furniture made before 1960 may contain lead paint. Before sanding, do a swab test with a lead paint testing kit. If lead is detected, strip chemically rather than sanding. Work outside or in a well-ventilated space, wear FFP3 mask and nitrile gloves.

For varnish or paint on a piece with no lead concern: apply a solvent-based or water-based chemical stripper generously with a paintbrush. Leave for 15 to 30 minutes. Scrape off the lifted finish with a plastic scraper; metal scrapers risk gouging the wood. Apply a second coat for thick paint layers. Wash down with white spirit or water and allow to dry fully.

Danish oil: easy to apply with a cloth. Dries to a low-sheen finish that feeds the wood and is repairable by adding a fresh coat. Good for bare wood tables and chairs. 500 ml costs around 10 pounds and covers a dining chair twice.
Chalk paint: adheres directly to most surfaces without sanding or priming. Matt finish; requires a wax topcoat for durability. Good for decorative pieces that do not take wear.
Satin water-based paint: primer plus two topcoats on a sanded and cleaned surface. Most durable option for pieces that take daily contact. Dries to a harder finish than chalk paint.

attrs.symptom: Chemical stripper not lifting the paint

content.text: The paint is a waterborne acrylic or modern trade paint that resists solvent strippers. Switch to a gel-based stripper, apply thickly. And cover with cling film to extend the dwell time to 1 to 2 hours.

attrs.symptom: Wood grain raised and rough after stripping

content.text: Normal: water-based stripper raises the grain. Sand lightly with 120-grit sandpaper in the direction of the grain before finishing. The raised grain disappears after one sand and one finish coat.
Step-by-step instructions for Assessing and refurbishing second-hand furniture go here.
